evals.name: factory-method-detection
evals.expected_output: Should identify Factory Method pattern as appropriate solution to eliminate switch statement and allow extension without modification

assertions.name: Explains why it fits
assertions.check: Output explains that Factory Method lets subclasses decide which notifier to create

assertions.name: Shows implementation approach
assertions.check: Output provides concrete steps or code for implementing the pattern

evals.name: no-pattern-needed
evals.expected_output: Should advise against using a design pattern - a simple method is sufficient

assertions.name: Advises against pattern
assertions.check: Output indicates no design pattern is needed for this simple case

assertions.name: Recommends simple solution
assertions.check: Output suggests a simple getFullName() method is the right approach

assertions.name: Explains reasoning
assertions.check: Output explains why patterns would be over-engineering here

evals.name: strategy-pattern-selection
evals.prompt: I'm building a payment processing system that needs to handle multiple payment methods (credit card, PayPal, crypto, bank transfer). Each has different validation rules and processing logic. Currently I have a PaymentProcessor with a massive processPayment method that has nested if-else statements. I need to be able to easily add new payment methods and switch between them at runtime based on user selection. What's the best approach?
evals.expected_output: Should identify Strategy pattern as the best fit, potentially mentioning Factory Method as complementary

assertions.name: Identifies Strategy pattern
assertions.check: Output recommends Strategy pattern for encapsulating payment algorithms

assertions.name: Explains runtime switching
assertions.check: Output explains how Strategy enables switching payment methods at runtime

assertions.name: Mentions open/closed benefit
assertions.check: Output mentions that new payment methods can be added without modifying existing code

evals.name: decorator-vs-inheritance
evals.prompt: I have a Coffee class and I need to support many combinations of add-ons: milk, sugar, cream, caramel, vanilla, whipped cream, etc. Currently I have subclasses like CoffeeWithMilk, CoffeeWithMilkAndSugar, CoffeeWithCreamAndCaramel, etc. I already have 20+ classes and it's getting unmanageable. How should I restructure this?
evals.expected_output: Should identify Decorator pattern as the solution to avoid class explosion

assertions.name: Identifies Decorator pattern
assertions.check: Output recommends Decorator pattern for this scenario

assertions.name: Explains class explosion problem
assertions.check: Output explains why inheritance causes exponential class growth

assertions.name: Shows composition approach
assertions.check: Output demonstrates wrapping components to add behavior dynamically

evals.name: observer-for-events
evals.prompt: I have an OrderService that needs to notify multiple systems when an order is placed: InventoryService needs to update stock, EmailService needs to send confirmation, AnalyticsService needs to track the event, LoyaltyService needs to update points, and there might be more services added in the future. Right now OrderService directly calls all these services, which makes it tightly coupled. How can I decouple this?
evals.expected_output: Should identify Observer pattern as the solution for event notification

assertions.name: Identifies Observer pattern
assertions.check: Output recommends Observer pattern for decoupling event notifications

assertions.name: Explains subscription mechanism
assertions.check: Output explains how services can subscribe to order events

assertions.name: Mentions loose coupling benefit
assertions.check: Output explains how this reduces coupling between OrderService and dependent services
